category_title: Elementary student with math learning disabilities who relies on semiconcrete reasoning
student_description: Chris is an eager but slow-processing student who relies heavily on semiconcrete strategies like finger counting and drawing. He struggles significantly with abstract mathematical sentences and gets easily confused when generalizing concepts across different real-world contexts (e.g., mapping 'flowers and boxes' to 'towers and cubes'). He uniquely requires explicit concrete-to-abstract sequencing, opportunities to verbalize his counting to self-correct, and direct mapping of diverse contexts to a single conceptual model.

criteria.rationale: Chris relies heavily on lower-level strategies and needs a structured, multi-stage pathway to develop advanced abstract operations without mimicking procedures.
criteria.check: The tutor's long-term plan explicitly sequences instruction from concrete objects to representational drawings, and finally to abstract symbols.

criteria.criterion_name: Cross-Context Mapping to a Single Model
criteria.rationale: Chris gets confused when problem contexts change (e.g., flowers/boxes vs. towers/cubes), so he needs help generalizing a single conceptual model across diverse scenarios.
criteria.check: The tutor incorporates a variety of real-world contextualized problems and explicitly maps them back to a consistent conceptual equation (e.g., Unit rate x # of Units = Product).

criteria.rationale: Chris will not naturally deduce abstract algorithms from drawing pictures; he requires direct instruction to bridge his semiconcrete methods to abstract math.
criteria.check: The tutor plans for explicit, direct instruction on how to translate semiconcrete models into abstract equations, rather than relying on implicit discovery.
